did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and transfer learning methods for the multi-class classification of skin lesions. Specifically, it implemented and fine-tuned pre-trained convolutional neural network architectures including MobileNetV2, Xception, and DenseNet on the ISIC 2019 dataset to classify dermoscopic images into eight diagnostic categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation, fine-tuning, and comparative analysis of deep learning models (MobileNetV2, Xception, DenseNet) on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It reports specific results such as accuracy metrics (99%) and discusses methodology including data augmentation and hyperparameter tuning. Although published in conference proceedings, it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multiclass Classification of Skin Lesions from Imbalanced Dataset A Comparative Analysis of Pretrained Transfer Learning Models
journal: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS-2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of multiclass skin lesion classification using the imbalanced ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains 25,331 dermoscopic images across nine diagnostic categories. The researchers implemented and fine-tuned seven pre-trained deep learning models, including MobileNetV2, Xception, DenseNet, VGG16, VGG19, InceptionV3, and AlexNet, to classify lesions into eight selected classes. To mitigate class imbalance, extensive preprocessing and data augmentation were applied, resulting in a balanced dataset with 12,873 instances per class. The models were evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with the dataset split into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing sets. MobileNetV2 demonstrated superior performance, achieving an initial accuracy of 96% which was enhanced to 99% through systematic hyperparameter tuning. The results indicate that deep learning models, particularly fine-tuned MobileNetV2, can effectively manage imbalanced datasets and provide high classification accuracy for clinical decision support.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the eight selected diagnostic categories from the ISIC 2019 dataset used for multiclass classification. The paper notes misclassifications between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus.

all_task_types.target: Eight diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The study implements and fine-tunes pre-trained deep learning models (MobileNetV2, Xception, DenseNet201, etc.) to predict discrete diagnostic labels. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. MobileNetV2 achieved 99% testing accuracy after hyperparameter tuning.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 25,331 dermoscopic images categorized into nine diagnostic classes, sourced from the ISIC 2019 challenge and prior years (2018). The study utilized a subset of this dataset, balancing it via augmentation to 12,873 instances per class.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (MobileNetV2, Xception, DenseNet, etc.) after balancing the dataset via augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation (10% of the balanced dataset, 10,298 instances) to monitor model performance during training.
use.testing: Used for final testing (10% of the balanced dataset, 10,299 instances) to evaluate the final model performance.
use.other: The original dataset contained 9 classes including 'None of the above', but this class was excluded from the study's eight selected classes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for model training and fine-tuning to minimize loss during the learning process.

statistical_methods_used.method: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) with momentum of 0.9
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for training specific models (DenseNet201, MobileNetV2) with a learning rate of 0.001 regulated by ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler.
Used to prevent overfitting by stopping training when validation performance ceased to improve.
Used for regularization to reduce overfitting.
Used to mitigate class imbalance and improve model robustness by creating a balanced dataset with 12,873 instances per class.
Used to evaluate classification performance, identify misclassifications (e.g., Melanoma vs. Melanocytic Nevus), and assess model confidence for specific lesion types.
Used as evaluation metrics to compare the performance of different deep learning models (Xception, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, Tuned MobileNetV2).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and comparisons were made based on these metric values without statistical significance testing.

Xception.notes: High F1-score (1.0) for Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesions. F1-score of 0.79 for Melanoma and 0.80 for Melanocytic Nevus.

DenseNet201.notes: Better performance on Benign Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma. Recall decreased for Melanoma (0.83) and Melanocytic Nevus (0.78).

MobileNetV2.notes: Best among initial models. F1-score of 0.98 for Melanoma and 0.99 for Basal Cell Carcinoma.

TunedMobileNetV2.notes: F1-scores of 1.0 for Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesions. F1-score of 0.96 for Melanocytic Nevus. Small misclassifications of 33 cases for MEL and 31 cases for NV.

ISIC 2019 Dataset.notes: MobileNetV2 exhibited superior classification performance, attaining an initial accuracy of 96%, subsequently enhanced to 99% through systematic hyperparameter tuning. The close alignment between training and validation accuracies suggests effective mitigation of overfitting and underfitting.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV2, Xception, DenseNet201, VGG16, VGG19, InceptionV3, AlexNet, CNN, ConvNeXtBase

model_characteristics.VGG19: 19 layers, dense architecture, high parameter count
model_characteristics.DenseNet201: Dense connectivity for better feature reuse

training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to mitigate class imbalance, resulting in balanced dataset of 12,873 instances per class
training_details.hyperparameter_tuning: Systematic hyperparameter tuning applied to MobileNetV2

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to mitigate class imbalance (resulting in a balanced dataset of 12,873 instances per class)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to mitigate class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset. The augmentation process resulted in a balanced dataset with 12,873 instances per class for the eight selected diagnostic categories.

conclusions: MobileNetV2 demonstrated superior classification performance among the tested pre-trained models (Xception, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2), achieving an initial testing accuracy of 96%.

Systematic hyperparameter tuning of MobileNetV2 significantly enhanced its performance, resulting in a testing accuracy of 99%, with training and validation accuracies of 99.87% and 98.92% respectively.
The close alignment between training and validation accuracies for the tuned MobileNetV2 indicates effective mitigation of overfitting and underfitting.
Deep learning models, particularly fine-tuned MobileNetV2, are capable of managing imbalanced datasets and achieving high classification accuracy, contributing to reliable clinical decision support.

The study highlights the challenge of misclassification between Melanoma (MEL) and Melanocytic Nevus (NV) due to their visual similarity, with small misclassifications of 33 and 31 cases respectively observed in the tuned model.
The models achieved almost perfect classification (F1-score of 1.0) for Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesions.

Future work is needed to enhance data diversity, improve model transparency, and incorporate explainable AI techniques to address limitations regarding limited model interpretability and develop robust systems for real-world clinical use.
important_remarks: The study artificially balanced the highly imbalanced ISIC 2019 dataset via augmentation to 12,873 instances per class, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence and could inflate performance metrics.
The paper lacks external validation on independent datasets or diverse skin-tone distributions, limiting the assessment of robustness against domain shifts.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the tuned MobileNetV2 model over others.
The model exhibits persistent misclassification between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus due to visual similarity, highlighting a critical safety concern for high-risk lesion detection.
The study does not address model interpretability or transparency, which are essential for clinical trust and adoption in dermatological decision support.
The dataset split (80/10/10) was applied after balancing, potentially introducing data leakage if augmentation techniques were not strictly confined to the training fold.
The paper acknowledges limited dataset diversity and suggests future work on domain adaptation, indicating current generalizability concerns.
